en_definition: the distance an object travels per unit of time, without regard to direction
csca_example: Calculate the speed of a car that travels 150 km in 2 hours.

en_definition: the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken
csca_example: Find the average speed of a runner who covers 400 m in 50 seconds.

en_definition: the velocity of an object at a single specific moment in time
csca_example: Use the gradient of the tangent on a distance-time graph to find the instantaneous velocity at t = 3 s.

en_definition: the single velocity that results from combining two or more velocities acting on an object
csca_example: Find the resultant velocity of a boat crossing a river with a current of 2 m/s.

en_definition: the maximum constant velocity reached by an object falling through a fluid when air resistance balances weight
csca_example: Explain why a skydiver eventually reaches terminal velocity.

en_definition: a decrease in speed over time, often called negative acceleration
csca_example: Calculate the deceleration of a car that slows from 20 m/s to rest in 4 seconds.

en_definition: a graph that shows how the distance travelled by an object changes over time
csca_example: Use a distance-time graph to determine when the object was stationary.

en_definition: a graph that shows how the velocity of an object changes over time
csca_example: Use a velocity-time graph to calculate the distance travelled between t = 0 and t = 5 s.

en_definition: a set of formulas relating displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time for uniformly accelerated motion
csca_example: Use the equations of motion to find the final velocity of an object accelerating at 3 m/s² from rest for 5 seconds.

en_definition: the horizontal distance travelled by a projectile before it lands
csca_example: Calculate the range of a projectile launched at 30° with an initial speed of 20 m/s.

en_definition: the total time a projectile remains in the air before landing
csca_example: Find the time of flight of a ball thrown vertically upward at 15 m/s.

en_definition: the greatest vertical height reached by a projectile during its flight
csca_example: Calculate the maximum height reached by a projectile launched at 25 m/s at an angle of 45°.

en_definition: a physical quantity that has magnitude only, with no direction
csca_example: State whether temperature is a scalar quantity or a vector quantity.

en_definition: a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction
csca_example: Explain why displacement is a vector quantity while distance is not.

en_definition: the single force equivalent to the combined effect of all forces acting on an object
csca_example: Calculate the resultant force acting on an object pulled by 10 N east and 4 N west.

en_definition: the overall force acting on an object after all individual forces have been combined
csca_example: Find the net force on a 5 kg object accelerating at 2 m/s².

en_definition: a state in which the resultant force acting on an object is zero
csca_example: Show that the object is in equilibrium by checking that the forces sum to zero.

en_definition: the tendency of an object to resist a change in its state of motion
csca_example: Explain why passengers lurch forward due to inertia when a car brakes suddenly.

en_definition: a diagram showing all the forces acting on a single object, drawn as arrows
csca_example: Draw a free body diagram for a box resting on an inclined plane.

en_definition: the frictional force exerted by air on an object moving through it
csca_example: Explain how air resistance affects the motion of a falling parachutist.

en_definition: the resistive force exerted on an object as it moves through a fluid such as air or water
csca_example: Explain how drag force increases with speed for an object moving through air.

en_definition: a number representing the ratio of the friction force between two surfaces to the normal force pressing them together
csca_example: Calculate the coefficient of friction between a block and a surface given a friction force of 8 N and a normal force of 20 N.

en_definition: the friction force that prevents an object from starting to move
csca_example: Explain why static friction must be overcome before a box begins to slide.

en_definition: the friction force that acts on an object already in motion
csca_example: Calculate the kinetic friction acting on a sliding block with a coefficient of friction of 0.3.

en_definition: the pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable
csca_example: Calculate the tension force in a rope supporting a hanging 5 kg mass.

en_definition: the force a surface exerts perpendicular to an object resting on it
csca_example: Calculate the normal reaction force on a 10 kg box resting on a horizontal table.

en_definition: a force that is directly exerted on an object by a person or another object
csca_example: Determine the applied force needed to push a 20 kg crate across the floor at constant velocity.

en_definition: a law stating that every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them
csca_example: Use Newton's law of gravitation to calculate the gravitational force between two 1000 kg masses 2 m apart.

en_definition: the force per unit mass experienced by an object in a gravitational field
csca_example: State the gravitational field strength near the surface of the Earth.

en_definition: the turning effect of a force about a pivot point, calculated as force multiplied by perpendicular distance
csca_example: Calculate the moment of a 20 N force applied 0.5 m from a pivot.

en_definition: the rotational equivalent of force, causing an object to rotate about an axis
csca_example: Calculate the torque produced by a 15 N force applied at a perpendicular distance of 0.3 m from the axis.

en_definition: a rule stating that for a body in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments equals the sum of anticlockwise moments
csca_example: Use the principle of moments to find the unknown weight on a balanced seesaw.

en_definition: the maximum extension a material can undergo and still return to its original shape when the force is removed
csca_example: Explain what happens to a spring when it is stretched beyond its elastic limit.

en_definition: a law stating that the extension of a spring is directly proportional to the force applied, up to its elastic limit
csca_example: Use Hooke's law to calculate the force needed to extend a spring by 0.05 m with a spring constant of 200 N/m.

en_definition: a measure of the stiffness of a spring, equal to the force needed to produce a unit extension
csca_example: Calculate the spring constant of a spring that extends 0.1 m under a force of 25 N.

en_definition: the energy transferred when a force moves an object through a distance
csca_example: Calculate the work done when a 50 N force moves an object 4 m in the direction of the force.

en_definition: the sum of the kinetic and potential energy of an object
csca_example: Calculate the total mechanical energy of a ball at a height of 10 m moving at 3 m/s.

en_definition: the energy stored in an object due to its height above a reference point in a gravitational field
csca_example: Calculate the gravitational potential energy of a 2 kg object raised 5 m above the ground.

en_definition: the energy stored in an object when it is stretched or compressed elastically
csca_example: Calculate the elastic potential energy stored in a spring extended 0.2 m with a spring constant of 150 N/m.

en_definition: a law stating that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred or transformed from one form to another
csca_example: Use the law of conservation of energy to find the speed of a ball at the bottom of a slide.

en_definition: the process by which energy moves from one object or form to another
csca_example: Describe the energy transfer that occurs when a ball is dropped and bounces.

en_definition: the proportion of input energy that is usefully transferred by a device, often expressed as a percentage
csca_example: Calculate the efficiency of a motor that converts 500 J of electrical energy into 350 J of useful kinetic energy.

en_definition: the rate at which a device or system produces or transfers energy
csca_example: Calculate the power output of a motor that lifts a 10 kg mass 3 m in 5 seconds.

en_definition: a diagram that uses arrows of different widths to show how energy is transferred and where it is wasted
csca_example: Draw a Sankey diagram to show the energy transfers in a filament lamp.

en_definition: the SI unit of power, equal to one joule of energy transferred per second
csca_example: Explain what it means for a light bulb to be rated at 60 watts.

en_definition: the SI unit of energy or work, equal to the work done by a force of one newton moving an object one metre
csca_example: State how many joules of work are done when a 1 N force moves an object 1 m.

en_definition: the product of an object's mass and velocity
csca_example: Calculate the linear momentum of a 1200 kg car travelling at 20 m/s.

en_definition: a law stating that the total momentum of a closed system remains constant if no external force acts on it
csca_example: Use conservation of momentum to find the final velocity after two trolleys collide and stick together.

en_definition: a collision in which both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved
csca_example: Determine whether the collision between the two billiard balls is an elastic collision.

en_definition: a collision in which momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not, often because objects stick together
csca_example: Calculate the kinetic energy lost in an inelastic collision between two colliding trolleys.

en_definition: the change in momentum of an object, equal to the force applied multiplied by the time it acts
csca_example: Calculate the impulse delivered to a ball struck by a bat for 0.01 s with an average force of 300 N.

en_definition: the difference between an object's final momentum and its initial momentum
csca_example: Calculate the change in momentum of a 0.5 kg ball that bounces off a wall, reversing its velocity of 4 m/s.

en_definition: the movement of an object along a circular path at a constant distance from a fixed centre point
csca_example: Describe the circular motion of a satellite orbiting the Earth.

en_definition: the force directed toward the centre of a circular path that keeps an object moving in that circle
csca_example: Calculate the centripetal force required to keep a 2 kg mass moving in a circle of radius 0.5 m at 4 m/s.

en_definition: the acceleration directed toward the centre of a circular path, caused by the changing direction of velocity
csca_example: Calculate the centripetal acceleration of an object moving at 6 m/s in a circle of radius 3 m.

en_definition: the rate at which an object rotates or sweeps through an angle, measured in radians per second
csca_example: Calculate the angular velocity of a wheel completing one revolution every 2 seconds.

en_definition: a wave in which the particles vibrate at right angles to the direction the wave travels
csca_example: Explain why light is classified as a transverse wave.

en_definition: a wave in which the particles vibrate parallel to the direction the wave travels
csca_example: Explain why sound is classified as a longitudinal wave.

en_definition: a region in a longitudinal wave where particles are pushed close together
csca_example: Label the compression regions on the diagram of a sound wave.

en_definition: a region in a longitudinal wave where particles are spread further apart
csca_example: Label the rarefaction regions on the diagram of a sound wave.

en_definition: an equation relating the speed of a wave to its frequency and wavelength
csca_example: Use the wave equation to find the speed of a wave with frequency 50 Hz and wavelength 2 m.

en_definition: the perceived highness or lowness of a sound, determined by its frequency
csca_example: Explain why a higher frequency sound wave has a higher pitch.

en_definition: the perceived strength of a sound, related to the amplitude of the sound wave
csca_example: Explain why increasing the amplitude of a sound wave increases its loudness.

en_definition: the complete reflection of a light ray inside a medium when it hits a boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle
csca_example: Explain how total internal reflection allows light to travel through an optical fibre.

en_definition: the angle of incidence above which total internal reflection occurs
csca_example: Calculate the critical angle for light travelling from glass into air, given a refractive index of 1.5.

en_definition: a measure of how much a material bends light, defined as the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to its speed in the material
csca_example: Calculate the refractive index of a medium in which light travels at 2 × 10⁸ m/s.

en_definition: the angle between an incoming ray of light and the normal at the point of contact
csca_example: Measure the angle of incidence of the light ray striking the mirror.

en_definition: a lens that curves inward and causes light rays to spread apart
csca_example: Explain why a concave lens produces a virtual, diminished image.

en_definition: a lens that curves outward and causes light rays to converge
csca_example: Use ray diagrams to show how a convex lens focuses parallel rays of light.

en_definition: the distance between the centre of a lens or mirror and its focal point
csca_example: Calculate the focal length of a lens given its power in dioptres.

en_definition: an image formed where light rays appear to diverge from, which cannot be projected onto a screen
csca_example: Explain why a plane mirror always forms a virtual image.

en_definition: the complete range of electromagnetic waves, ordered by wavelength and frequency
csca_example: List the seven regions of the electromagnetic spectrum in order of increasing frequency.

en_definition: electromagnetic radiation with a wavelength shorter than visible light but longer than X-rays
csca_example: Explain a harmful effect of excessive exposure to ultraviolet radiation.

en_definition: a wave pattern that appears stationary, formed by the interference of two waves travelling in opposite directions
csca_example: Describe how a standing wave is formed on a vibrating string.

en_definition: a large increase in amplitude that occurs when a system is driven at its natural frequency
csca_example: Explain why a bridge can be damaged by resonance caused by soldiers marching in step.

en_definition: the change in observed frequency of a wave caused by relative motion between the source and the observer
csca_example: Use the Doppler effect to explain why an ambulance siren sounds higher pitched as it approaches.

en_definition: the energy supplied by a source per unit charge, driving current around a circuit
csca_example: Calculate the electromotive force of a battery supplying 20 J of energy to move 4 C of charge.

en_definition: the amount of energy transferred per unit charge between two points in a circuit
csca_example: Calculate the potential difference across a resistor carrying a current of 2 A with resistance 5 Ω.

en_definition: a law stating that the current through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it, at constant temperature
csca_example: Use Ohm's law to find the current through a 10 Ω resistor connected to a 5 V supply.

en_definition: a component in a circuit that opposes the flow of electric current
csca_example: Calculate the total resistance of two 4 Ω resistors connected in series.

en_definition: an instrument used to measure potential difference, connected in parallel across a component
csca_example: Explain why a voltmeter must be connected in parallel to measure potential difference accurately.

en_definition: a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force in an electric field
csca_example: Calculate the electrical charge that flows when a current of 3 A passes for 10 seconds.

en_definition: a material that allows electric current to flow easily through it
csca_example: Explain why copper is used as a conductor in electrical wiring.

en_definition: a material that does not allow electric current to flow through it easily
csca_example: Explain why rubber is used as an insulator around electrical wires.

en_definition: the rate at which electrical energy is transferred in a circuit
csca_example: Calculate the electrical power of a device operating at 12 V with a current of 3 A.

en_definition: the combined resistance of all the components in a circuit
csca_example: Calculate the total resistance of a 6 Ω and a 3 Ω resistor connected in parallel.

en_definition: a safety device containing a thin wire that melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes too high
csca_example: Explain why a fuse is fitted in the live wire of a plug.

en_definition: an electric current that flows in one direction only
csca_example: State an example of a device that uses direct current.

en_definition: an electric current that regularly reverses direction
csca_example: Explain why the mains electricity supply uses alternating current.

en_definition: a measure of a component's ability to store electric charge per unit potential difference
csca_example: Calculate the capacitance of a capacitor storing 6 mC of charge at 12 V.

en_definition: the force exerted between magnetic poles or on a moving charge in a magnetic field
csca_example: Describe the magnetic force between two like poles of a magnet.

en_definition: a magnet created by passing an electric current through a coil of wire, often wound around an iron core
csca_example: Explain how increasing the current strengthens an electromagnet.

en_definition: a coil of wire that produces a uniform magnetic field inside it when a current flows through it
csca_example: Describe the magnetic field produced inside a solenoid carrying a current.

en_definition: the force experienced by a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field
csca_example: Use the motor effect to explain why a current-carrying wire moves when placed between magnetic poles.

en_definition: a rule used to predict the direction of the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field
csca_example: Use Fleming's left-hand rule to find the direction of the force on the wire.

en_definition: a device that converts electrical energy into kinetic energy using the motor effect
csca_example: Explain how a simple electric motor produces continuous rotation.

en_definition: a device that converts kinetic energy into electrical energy using electromagnetic induction
csca_example: Explain how a simple electric generator produces an alternating current.

en_definition: a device that changes the voltage of an alternating current using two coils linked by a magnetic field
csca_example: Calculate the output voltage of a step-up transformer given the primary voltage and the turns ratio.

en_definition: the amount of energy needed to raise the temperature of an object by one degree Celsius
csca_example: Calculate the heat capacity of an object that requires 800 J to raise its temperature by 4°C.

en_definition: the energy absorbed or released during a change of state without a change in temperature
csca_example: Calculate the latent heat required to melt 2 kg of ice.

en_definition: the transfer of thermal energy through a material by the vibration and collision of particles
csca_example: Explain why metals are good conductors of heat through conduction.

en_definition: the transfer of thermal energy through a fluid by the movement of warmer, less dense regions rising
csca_example: Explain how convection currents form in a heated pan of water.

en_definition: the transfer of thermal energy by electromagnetic waves, which can travel through a vacuum
csca_example: Explain how radiation allows heat from the Sun to reach the Earth.

en_definition: a material that resists the flow of thermal energy through it
csca_example: Explain why fibreglass is used as a thermal insulator in loft insulation.

en_definition: the increase in size of a material as its temperature rises
csca_example: Explain why a gap is left between railway track sections to allow for thermal expansion.

en_definition: the lowest possible temperature, at which particles have minimum thermal energy, equal to -273°C
csca_example: Convert a temperature of 25°C into kelvin, given that absolute zero is -273°C.

en_definition: a theory explaining the behaviour of solids, liquids, and gases in terms of the motion and arrangement of particles
csca_example: Use the kinetic theory of matter to explain why gases expand to fill their container.

en_definition: the random, erratic movement of small particles suspended in a fluid, caused by collisions with fluid molecules
csca_example: Explain how Brownian motion provides evidence for the kinetic theory of matter.

en_definition: the process by which particles escape from the surface of a liquid to become a gas, below boiling point
csca_example: Explain why evaporation causes a cooling effect on the skin.

en_definition: the temperature at which a liquid changes into a gas throughout its whole volume
csca_example: State the boiling point of water at standard atmospheric pressure.

en_definition: the temperature at which a solid changes into a liquid
csca_example: State the melting point of ice at standard atmospheric pressure.

en_definition: a law stating that at constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed mass of gas is inversely proportional to its volume
csca_example: Use Boyle's law to find the new pressure of a gas when its volume is halved at constant temperature.

en_definition: the pressure exerted by the weight of the air in the atmosphere
csca_example: Explain why atmospheric pressure decreases with increasing altitude.

en_definition: the pressure exerted by a liquid due to its weight, increasing with depth
csca_example: Calculate the liquid pressure at a depth of 10 m in water of density 1000 kg/m³.

en_definition: the upward force exerted by a fluid on an object partly or fully submerged in it
csca_example: Calculate the upthrust on an object that displaces 0.002 m³ of water.

en_definition: a principle stating that the upthrust on an object in a fluid equals the weight of fluid displaced
csca_example: Use Archimedes' principle to explain why a ship made of steel can float.

en_definition: a unit of measurement from the International System of Units, used as a global scientific standard
csca_example: State the SI unit for measuring electric current.

en_definition: the range within which the true value of a measurement is likely to lie
csca_example: State the uncertainty in a length measurement taken with a ruler marked in millimetres.

en_definition: a consistent error that affects every measurement in the same way, often due to faulty equipment
csca_example: Explain how a zero error on a balance can cause a systematic error.

en_definition: an error that varies unpredictably between measurements, often due to human reaction time or fluctuating conditions
csca_example: Explain how repeating a measurement several times reduces the effect of random error.

en_definition: how close a measured value is to the true or accepted value
csca_example: Explain why a thermometer that always reads 2°C too high lacks accuracy.

en_definition: an instrument used to measure small lengths precisely, including internal and external dimensions
csca_example: Use a vernier caliper to measure the diameter of a small metal ball.

en_definition: a tall, narrow container marked with a scale, used to measure the volume of a liquid
csca_example: Use a measuring cylinder to measure out 50 cm³ of water.

en_definition: an instrument used to measure temperature
csca_example: Use a thermometer to record the temperature of the water every minute.

en_definition: an insulated container used to measure the heat energy released or absorbed during a physical or chemical process
csca_example: Use a calorimeter to measure the specific heat capacity of the metal block.

en_definition: a sensor used to measure the speed or timing of an object as it passes through a beam of light
csca_example: Use a light gate to measure the velocity of the trolley as it passes through.

en_definition: a factor that is kept constant during an experiment so it does not affect the results
csca_example: State one control variable that should be kept constant in the resistance experiment.

en_definition: taking a measurement more than once to check for consistency and reduce the effect of random error
csca_example: Explain why repeat readings improve the reliability of the results.

en_definition: to mark points on a set of axes to represent collected data and often join them with a line or curve
csca_example: Plot a graph of extension against force for the spring experiment.

en_definition: to carry out a systematic experiment in order to test a hypothesis or find a relationship
csca_example: Investigate how the length of a pendulum affects its period of oscillation.
